coredata.dc:title: Isothermal sections of the Ti–Co–Sn and Ti–Co–Sb systems
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2004 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Stadnyk, Yu.
Romaka, L.
Horyn, A.
Tkachuk, A.
Gorelenko, Yu.
Rogl, P.
coredata.dc:description:
Isothermal sections at 870 and 1070K have been investigated for the Ti–Co–Sn and Ti–Co–Sb ternary systems. Phase relations in the Ti–Co–Sn system are characterized by the presence of extended solid solutions Ti5Co x Sn3 (x = 0–1.0) and TiCo2-
x Sn (x = 0–1.0). For the ternary Ti5Co0.46Sb2.54 compound the crystal structure was refined from X-ray powder diffraction data by the Rietveld method (W5Si3-type structure, space group I4/mcm, Z = 4, a = 1.0474(1), c = 0.52415(1)nm).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phase diagram
Intermetallics
Crystal structure
Stannides
Antimonides

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Isothermal sections of the Ti–Co–Sn system at 870 and 1070K.
Isothermal sections of the Ti–Co–Sb system at 870 and 1070K.
The projection of the Ti5Co0.46Sb2.54 structure viewed along the tetragonal axis and coordination polyhedra of the atoms.
EMPA image of the TiCoSb sample.
AFM image of the TiCoSb sample annealed at 1070K.
AFM image of the as-cast TiCoSb sample.
Interatomic distances for the Ti5Co0.46Sb2.54 compound

article.ce:title: Isothermal sections of the Ti–Co–Sn and Ti–Co–Sb systems

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Inorganic Chemistry Department, Ivan Franko National University, Kyryl & Mefodiy Str. 6, 79005 L’viv, Ukraine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Alberta, Edmonton, AB, Canada T6G 2G2

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institut fur Physikalische Chemie, Universitat Wien, Wahringerstrasse 42, A-1090 Wien, Austria

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Isothermal sections at 870 and 1070K have been investigated for the Ti–Co–Sn and Ti–Co–Sb ternary systems. Phase relations in the Ti–Co–Sn system are characterized by the presence of extended solid solutions Ti5Co x Sn3 (x = 0–1.0) and TiCo2-
x Sn (x = 0–1.0). For the ternary Ti5Co0.46Sb2.54 compound the crystal structure was refined from X-ray powder diffraction data by the Rietveld method (W5Si3-type structure, space group I4/mcm, Z = 4, a = 1.0474(1), c = 0.52415(1)nm).

ce:section.ce:para:
The interaction of the IVb-group elements Ti, Zr, and Hf with cobalt or nickel and tin within ternary systems had been sufficiently investigated before [1–6]. Ternary intermetallic compounds especially with equiatomic composition were found in all these systems. The stannides TiNiSn, ZrNiSn, HfNiSn show semiconducting behavior despite the presence of three metals in the chemical formulae [7,8]. The TiCoSb compound has similar transport properties as well [9,10]. The crystal structure and physical properties of the TiCo2−
x Sn compound had been reported in [11].

The main goal of the present work is to study the interaction of the components in the Ti–Co–Sn and Ti–Co–Sb ternary systems, to construct the isothermal sections of the phase diagrams and to refine the crystal structure of the ternary Ti5Co0.46Sb2.54 compound. The information concerning the binary boundary systems Ti–Co, Ti–Sn, Ti–Sb, Co–Sn, and Co–Sb was taken from [12,13].

Samples for investigation were prepared by direct arc melting of the pure elements (overall purity of the constituents was more than 99.99wt.%) using an inconsumable tungsten electrode under a high purity Ti-gettered argon atmosphere on a water-cooled copper hearth. Weight loss of the charges was generally less than 1wt.%. The ingots were annealed in vacuum-sealed quartz tubes at 870 or 1070K for 720h and then quenched in cold water. Phase analyses were carried out using Debye–Scherrer X-ray powder film data (RKD-57.3 camera, CrK-radiation) or powder patterns obtained from a DRON-2.0 diffractometer (Fe Kα-radiation). The intensity data for crystal structure refinement of Ti5Co0.46Sb2.54 were collected at room temperature from rotating samples on a Siemens D5000 powder diffractometer (Cu Kα-radiation, 20° ≤ 2θ ≤ 100° step scan mode with a step size of 0.05° and counting time of 20s per data point). The structure refinement was carried out with the full profile Rietveld method using the program FULLPROF [14]. The TiCoSb specimen mentioned in [9] was studied by electron microscope analysis (EMPA) on a Carl Zeiss DSM 962 instrument equipped with a Link EDX system operating with 20kV acceleration voltage and 60mA sample current and atomic force microscopy (NanoScope III, Digital Instruments, USA).

One hundred and seven alloys have been investigated for the determination of the phase equilibria in the Ti–Co–Sn system. The combined isothermal sections at 1070K between 0 and 55 at.% Sn and at 870K for more than 55 at.% Sn are presented in Fig. 1 . The existence of the two previously known compounds, TiCoSn with the MgAgAs structure type (ST) crystallizing in space group (SG) F
4 ¯
3m (a = 0.6003nm) and TiCo2Sn (ST MnCu2Al, SG Fm3m, a = 0.6067nm) [11,15,16] was confirmed. These two phases serve as the edge members of the solid solution TiCo2-
x Sn (x = 0–1.0). The magnetic and electro-transport properties and crystal structure of this solid solution were described previously [11]. At the annealing temperatures indicated, no further ternary compounds were observed. However, X-ray analysis revealed the existence of a solid solution, Ti5Co x Sn3, for x = 0–1.0, formed by cobalt insertion into centers of all titanium octahedral sites in the binary Ti5Sn3 (ST Mn5Si3-type). The corresponding lattice parameters change from a = 0.8052(2) to 0.8148(3) nm and from c = 0.5454(2) to 0.5533(1) nm. The filled structure is well known as the Ti5Ga4-type.

Phase equilibria in the Ti–Co–Sb system were studied by means of X-ray analysis of 115 ternary and binary alloys annealed at 870 and 1070K. The isothermal sections of the phase diagram are presented in Fig. 2 . The existence of the well-known compound TiCoSb (ST MgAgAs) [17] was confirmed. Electro-transport and magnetic properties as well as the crystal structure refinement of this compound were recently reported in [9]. Besides TiCoSb at the investigation temperature the ternary antimonide Ti5Co0.46Sb2.54 was confirmed in this system. For the first time this compound was reported in [18] and then was confirmed in the Ti5Co1-
x Sb2+
x composition [19]. We have also prepared the alloy of the nominal composition of Ti5CoSb3. Unfortunately, the expected ternary compound of Hf5CuSn3-type before reported in the literature [20] have not been observed after annealing at 1070K.

The above-mentioned ternary compound crystallizes in the W5Si3 structure type (SG I4/mcm, Z = 4) with a = 1.04742(2), c = 0.52415(1) nm. The crystal structure of the Ti5Co0.46Sb2.54 sample was refined from X-ray powder diffraction data by the Rietveld method using the FULLPROF program. The initial atomic distribution was taken as in the Ti5Ni0.45Sb2.55 structure [19]. Refinement of the occupancies resulted in a 0.46/0.54 distribution of Co/Sb atoms in the (4a) site and complete occupancy of the other sites, which is consistent with the Ti5Co0.46(2)Sb2.54(2) formula. In the final cycle of least-squares refinement a total of 26 parameters was optimized, including the scale factor, background, zero shift, lattice parameters, pseudo-Voigt peak profile parameters, occupancy parameter for the Co/Sb position, atomic coordinates, and the isotropic displacement parameters. The values of the residual factor R B, R P and R WP are equal to 4.15, 6.89 and 9.12, respectively. The atomic parameters, refined for 94 h
k
l reflections, are: Ti1 in 16(k): x y 0 (x = 0.0738(2), y = 0.2176(2)), B = 0.81(8); Ti2 in 4(b):
0
1 2
1 4
, B = 0.94(8); 0.46(2)Co + 0.54(2)Sb in 4(a): 00
1 4
,
B = 0.72(9); Sb in 8(h):
x
x +
1 2
0
(x = 0.1629(8)), B = 0.74(6). The interatomic distances (δ) for Ti5Co0.46Sb2.54 are given in Table 1 . Fig. 3 shows a projection of the Ti5Co0.46Sb2.54 unit cell on the XY plane and coordination polyhedra of the appropriate atoms, respectively. The structure of the Ti5Co0.46Sb2.54 compound can be described as based on two forms of columns running parallel to the c-direction. One column consists of M-centered Ti8 square antiprisms. The other column consists of Ti-centered [Sb4Ti8] hexagonal antiprisms. The central M and Ti2 atoms are connected to each other to form linear chains.

In [9] we reported that the magnetic properties of TiCoSb have a different magnetic behavior in as-cast and annealed states, though the diffraction patterns show pure single-phase state. The specific susceptibility of as-cast TiCoSb is 0.764 × 10−6 emu/g in an applied magnetic field of 10kG and room temperature. The annealed compound seems to behave like a magnetically ordered substance. Similar magnetization behavior was observed in [21] where the as-cast sample showed Pauli susceptibility. After heat treatment at 1070K for 100h this compound yielded a magnetic susceptibility nearly five times higher than before. Terada et al. [21,22] explained this behavior as being due to a small amount of a ferromagnetic phase precipitated after the heat treatment. The same TiCoSb specimen mentioned in [9] in as-cast and annealed states was studied using microscope analysis and the existence of any other phase was ruled out. The microstructure image of TiCoSb is presented in Fig. 4 . The micrographs show practically identical pictures for as-cast and annealed states. On the other hand, an investigation of the sample surface (Figs. 5 and 6
) using atomic force microscopy showed different morphologies of the surfaces. Investigations of the structure and the physical properties of the TiCoSb compound are in progress.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Ukrainian Ministry of Education and Science (grant 0103U001887).

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Yu.V. Stadnyk, L.P. Romaka, R.V. Skolozdra, ICT 98, Nagoya, Japan, 1998, p. SN-11.
R.V. Skolozdra, Stannides of the Rare Earths and Transition Metals, Svit, L’viv, 1993, p. 200 (in Ukrainian).
Yu.V. Stadnyk, A.M. Goryn, L.P. Romaka, Yu.K. Gorelenko, R.V. Skolozdra, Patent of the Ukraine, No. 25472A (1998).
T.B. Massalski, Binary Alloy Diagrams, ASM, Materials Park, OH, USA, 1998.
P. Villars, L.D. Calvert, Pearson's Handbook of Crystallographic Data for Intermetallic Phases, ASM, Materials Park, OH, USA, 1997.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: FULLPROF, a Program for Rietveld Refinement and Pattern Matching Analysis, version 3.5d, October 1998, LLB JRC (Saclay, France), based on the original code provided by D.B. Wiles, R.A. Young
